Preliminary results–NHS-Delay Feb 12

Author

Jane Lange

Objective

  • Examine the impact of a delay in diagnosis in clinically detected individuals on the outcomes of the NHS Galleri trial.

  • Examine sensitivity of results to the length of delay.

Methods

NHS Trial Simulation

  • We simulated 200 iterations of the trial.
  • Each NHS-Galleri simulation includes N=70,020 participants in both the screening and control arms, followed for three annual screens plus one additional year of follow-up. Starting ages range from 50 to 77 years, assigned according to UK demographic tables(Northern Ireland Statistics And Research Agency 2022). The simulation permits censoring due to other-cause mortality but assumes no additional dropout.
  • The trial was simulated using the MCEDsim package, which generates individual level natural histories according to a five-state model (Figure 1).
  • Outcomes are simulated for 11 solid cancer sites (anus, bladder, colorectal, esophagus, gastric, head and neck, liver, lung, lymphoma,ovary, pancreas), corresponding to 11/12 cancers targeted in the NHS Galleri trial endpoints.
  • For the NHS Galleri simulations, models are calibrated to National Cancer Registration and Analysis Service (NCRAS) data. [Need to rerun simulations with UK data–these results actually based on US data.]
  • Other cause death is based on US mortality tables.

Natural history and test performance inputs

  • MCEDsim is calibrated to age- and stage-specific cancer incidence data based on two key inputs: the overall mean pre-clinical sojourn time (OMST) and the late-stage mean pre-clinical sojourn time (LMST).
  • Natural history model and sensitivity inputs are specified as in (Gogebakan et al. 2025), which noted that the only existing study of pre-clinical detectability of the GRAIL demonstrated virtually no sensitivity beyond 2 years prior to clinical diagnosis(Patel et al. 2023).
  • Sensitivity of the MCED test for each cancer site is set so that early-stage sensitivity is either equal to that observed in case-control studies (Klein et al. 2021) of clinically diagnosed patients (optimistic) or 50% of that level (conservative) (Table 1). Sensitivity for late-stage disease is assumed to be equal to that of clinically diagnosed patients. The three combinations of OMST, LMST, and early stage sensitivity assumptions are summarized in Table 2.

Delays in diagnosis due to spillover effects

  • To operationalize the spillover effect, we add a delay to the times of diagnosis in those with clinically detected disease in screen and control arms.
  • Currently, we assume a constant delay across all individuals.
  • Specified delays range from 0-.5 years, in increments of .1 years.
  • Among individuals who would have been diagnosed at an early stage in the absence of delay, we resample stage at the delayed diagnosis time based on the probability of transitioning between early- and late-stage disease.
  • We assume a continuous-time Markov model for stage progression, with an average duration of 0.5 years spent in early-stage disease.

Reporting results

  • We report counts of early-stage (I-II) and late-stage disease detected at the end of the trial for screen- and clinically-detected cancers for the range of delay scenarios (0-.6 years).
  • We also report the absolute and relative reduction in late- stage disease in the screen and control arms.
  • Current results are based on outcomes observed within the trial (i.e., censored by the end of the trial).

Results

  • Under diagnostic delays in clinically diagnosed patients

    • Overall number of clinically detected cases observed during the trial goes down (Figure 2)

    • Counts of clinically diagnosed cancers in late-stage disease increase, and early-stage cancers decrease.

    • Counts of screen-detected cases are unaffected.

  • Despite the fact that late-stage cases increase in both the screen and control arms with delayed diagnosis, the screen arm increases are proportionally greater.
  • Thus, the relative reduction in late-stage disease tends to decrease with increasing delay
    • This pattern is likely due to the fact that in our simulation, in absence of a delay in diagnosis, early-stage disease is relatively more abundant in interval cancers (55%) than in clinically diagnosed cases in the control arm (43%). Thus, under diagnostic delays, more early-stage cases screen arm are subject to transitioning to late-stage disease by the time they are detected.

Open questions

  • How do we want to consider extended follow-up?

    • Of those cancers that are diagnosed within the trial, project final disease stage after X years of follow-up.
    • Assume trial is extended by X years, spillover effects continue, and project late-stage disease for all diagnosed within the full extended time frame.

Next steps

  • Rerun trial simulation using UK natural history models.
  • Generate results for extended follow-up.
  • Incorporate mortality outcomes?
  • Rerun results for different natural history assumptions.
  • Allow early-to-late transition rate to vary by cancer site.

References

Gogebakan, Kemal Caglar, Jane Lange, Lukas Owens, Amalie Pinderup, Roman Gulati, Larry G. Kessler, Georgios Lyratzopoulos, and Ruth Etzioni. 2025. “Clinical Significance of a Multicancer Screening Trial With Stage-Based End Points.” JAMA Network Open 8 (10): e2536247. https://doi.org/10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2025.36247.
Klein, E. A., D. Richards, A. Cohn, M. Tummala, R. Lapham, D. Cosgrove, G. Chung, et al. 2021. “Clinical Validation of a Targeted Methylation-Based Multi-Cancer Early Detection Test Using an Independent Validation Set.” Annals of Oncology : Official Journal of the European Society for Medical Oncology 32 (9): 1167–77. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.annonc.2021.05.806.
Northern Ireland Statistics And Research Agency. 2022. “2021 Census Aggegate Data First Release(Data Downloaded: May 2022).” Northern Ireland Statistics; Research Agency. https://doi.org/10.5257/CENSUS/AGGREGATE-2021-1.
Patel, Alpa, Christina A. Clarke Dur, Gregory Alexander, Emily L Deubler, Eric T. Fung, Roger Jiang, Cari Lichtman, et al. 2023. “Methylated DNA Biomarkers and Incident Cancer in the American Cancer Society (ACS) Cancer Prevention Study-3 (CPS-3) Cohort.” Journal of Clinical Oncology 41 (16_suppl): 3004–4. https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.2023.41.16_suppl.3004.
